text: It came back with very perfect prediction within the design space, the data that we had, and it's like, wow, that was quite easy. AI has made great progress over the last couple of years, not just in language models, but also in physical simulations, robotics, automotive, and industrial manufacturing. It's only been really in the last

Let's say six months that I think this space has gotten really exciting and you can create foundational physics models that predict simulation runs without having to do them. In this episode of AI Cloud Essentials, I sit down with Richard, who leads physical AI at Coreweave, and we talk about the rapidly evolving innovations in this field. Sometimes you have to get out of the AI space to make actual progress. Don't miss this conversation.

Hello, everyone. Welcome to season two of the AI Cloud Essentials, a podcast series brought to you by Core Read. Today, we are on episode two, and I'm super excited because I'm joined by Richard, and we are going to talk about how AI is breaking into the physical world, out of the digital terminal into the physical world. So welcome, Richard. Let's kick things off with...

Your journey in this area. How did you get into the physical simulation? What is so exciting about it? Would love to hear your perspective on it. Thanks, first of all, great to be here. I probably got into the whole AI for physics and physical systems long before ChatGPT, long before it was one of the biggest topics to talk about.

The world's leading conferences. Some people might say I've been too early. I think I just was in the right place half a decade too early. So I started a company right out of university. I was a PhD student at Imperial College in London, researching how to optimize aircraft engines with Rolls-Royce. I had a little stint at NASA where I worked on the Mars rocket, trying to optimize that. And then

did a research project actually in Stanford where I was supposed to model turbulence. And few people know turbulence is one of the most difficult problems in the world. It's almost impossible to solve. And I couldn't solve it either. Big surprise. And so I cheated.

I used AI. This was in 2016. It was a big conference in Stanford. The world's smartest people were there. Nobody could figure out these equations. And I was like, you know what I'm going to do? I'm going to take a little bit of data from some real wind tunnel. I'm going to fit a deep neural network on this, and I'm going to see what this predicts. And it came back with very perfect prediction within the design space, the data that we had. And it's like, wow, that was quite easy.

This is one of the hardest problems in the world. And with a couple of lines of code and a little bit of training, I could suddenly solve it. When I say I, AI could suddenly solve it. And so that's how I was hooked. I got in. I was like, amazing. I'm going to, from now on, work on AI for science and engineering. And so I did. Awesome.

Really your aha moment, right? So that this is going to be something really transformative. So I've been reading a lot about some of your success and proven wins and case specifics. It would be fantastic to hear, for example, if you could talk about some of the use cases where it really transformed the aha moment that you just talked about and how usage of those data and how those physical simulations really solving the problem.

So I think it's fair to say that I've tried everything you can with AI in engineering. Literally everything, right? Like when you, I started a startup on my company with the main idea, I want to do AI in science. And AI in science is everything. AI in science is aerospace engineering, rocket engineering, it's automotive, it's pharma, it's robotics, it's trying to figure out new medical equipment. And over the last nine years, I've literally tried everything.

And one of the first things that I was really excited by is the fact that physical simulations, the tools that large engineering companies use to avoid prototyping something and trying to understand just how they work in the virtual world before building something, tend to be very compute intensive and then tend to take a very long time. Right at the beginning, we used dynamic graph neural networks and they were very impressive. You could...

Very quickly impress a VP of engineering that he could run a simulation 10,000 times faster than they could ever do it before. And it was impressive, but the problem was that the economics of training versus inference didn't quite work out. People needed to train 1,500, 2,000 simulations to essentially predict one simulation run and then realize that that didn't quite work because they had to change the design too much.

Out of the learning space, and so they couldn't actually predict it, so they had to run another 500 simulations. So long story short, we were just too early because models weren't quite intelligent enough yet to actually make predictions and save people design iterations. That all changed massively when transformers were introduced, and suddenly you could actually train...
An AI model on 10, 15, 20 fairly complex simulations with huge design spaces and get a prediction for a future design run that actually helped you make a decision and was like...

And that only happened really recently. It's only been really in the last, let's say, six months that I think this space has gotten really exciting. And you can create foundational physics models that predict simulation runs without having to do them.

Yeah, that's fantastic to hear, Richard. And I think it also helps in kind of taking care of a lot of historical data, which in the past people couldn't have used it. Is that a fair statement? Because now when you're working with, you know, transformer models, which can process, you know, not just, you know, multiple simulations, but a lot of, you know, historical data to kind of predict the future much more accurately. Is that a fair statement?

I've always loved the idea of that. I've gone to all of the big engineering companies and I've asked them, hey, how many chassis simulations do you actually have? And it's like, oh, yeah, I think we've run 150,000 of those in the last six months alone, and we probably have a million in storage. And then we tried to build AI models that actually learn from them. And then you very quickly realize how much of a problem data.
Thank you for watching.

I think in general that is the idea, but in reality, just like with every historic data problem, it's remarkably hard to go and sort through all of this data and actually bring it together. And so with the latest transformer models, I don't think that is that much of an issue anymore, because you can literally come up with an intelligent model from fairly few designs and simulations, and people have
trying to come up with ways to bridge the gap. But yes, that's where the real value is and it has historically been very, very hard to unify all of this.

Yeah, that's a fantastic insight for our viewers today because, you know, we kind of assume that, you know, you have tons of data and you can make progress. So now let's pivot to a little bit more as to, because there's a lot of excitement in the industry about where the future is headed. The potential that we are talking about is not just limited to the physical simulations, but a lot of other industries. So I would love to get your take on what are the other industries, where are we actually venturing, and some of the hot happening items around physical robots.

would love to hear what you guys are up to and what are you seeing in the industry? So Monolith and I, I think we took a very different route to all of the other startups and even big engineering companies in this industry because we were too early for the simulation space. We basically gave up on simulations and instead focused on testing, which very different idea. You basically say, hey, look, everyone, we've tried to simulate what happens with this chassis or in this crash test.

It didn't work. The reality was more complex than any simulation that we can really run. So we're just going to go and have to test this anyway. And so we came up with this counterintuitive notion that simulations are good, but they will never be as good as the real world. And so instead of running simulations, why don't we directly include AI into the real world, learn from real physical data, real physical prototypes?

And this is where we actually started getting into robotics, because within robotics, one of the big topics out there is what people call the sim-to-real gap, where everyone is excited about how I can now run a million simulations of a robot on a cloud. But I've been arguing for a while that this doesn't really help you. If you ask a robot arm to essentially pick up a plastic bottle,
In virtual reality this plastic bottle is solid.

It's firm. It doesn't move. It's rigid. Because simulating how plastic crumbles when you touch it is remarkably hard and takes a huge amount of computational effort that you could run in two seconds. It's impossible. And so instead of doing this in virtual reality, basically you could do it in the real physical world. You could set up a robot, put it into a lab, and just practice with a water bottle and just see what does it really do. Like learning faster with fewer data points from real physical...

is not the same thing as the physical simulation world, but is a different area of machine learning where you're literally trying within control systems to just see how do I very quickly learn in real time, in a feedback loop, in a real world environment.

There's a couple of use cases that we've published as Monolith where these things have actually yielded great results. There's a case study published with Nissan where we got them off the path of, hey, let's try and simulate everything and just, hey, look, guys. In physical testing, there are very simple components in your systems, just like simple bolts, like you bolt together two metal components. Should be trivial, but turns out is surprisingly hard. But somewhere out there is specialists who spend 90 years trying to figure out

How to attach two metal plates with a bolt. And there's a lot of knowledge in this. There's a lot of hardware testing in this. I mean, okay, why don't we just take all of this hardware data, put it into a machine learning algorithm, and have it learn. If I'm going to go and build this, what's your recommendation? What's going to happen in the real world? No simulations, just learning from real world experience. And it turned out that worked really well. Like once Nissan started rolling this out,

For physical tests, they could reduce testing across all of their chassis by like 17%, which doesn't sound like a lot, but a lot of these tests are very critical for safety, so they couldn't be omitted.

Awesome, awesome. So as we are kind of expanding into the other industries and looking into, could you give us some perspective on what other industry segments are you kind of expanding into or looking into? You know, there's a lot of buzz in the industry about collaborative robots or humanoid robots. Are you looking into those areas as well? So for me, within physical AI, the biggest areas are number one, automotive.

Autonomous cars have made a huge leap in the last three months alone, in particular through releases like NVIDIA, Alpameo, where your car can now argue with you and say, no, I'm not going to go and turn left because there's a cyclist on the road there.

It stops that these AI models were black boxes. It's like, well, I don't trust my autonomous car because it's a black box. I don't know what it does. I don't trust it. They cannot argue back and explain in words why they've tried to do it in a specific way and even involve you in the process. So that and the fact that the enormous amount of compute that is now available, thanks to the AI boom, they can train autonomous cars in a completely different way.

Automotive autonomous cars I think are the number one area for me where I'm expecting big jumps. The second area that I think is really huge is manufacturing. I keep saying like when Jensen Huang goes on stage and says it's going to be a 50 trillion dollar industry by 2050 physical AI, I keep joking that out of that 50 trillion dollar probably 49 trillion

is in good old-fashioned manufacturing. Like, how do I create a Starbucks cup? Like, somebody needs to manufacture that. Somebody needs to check that the quality of this cup is actually all right. Like, there shouldn't be a leak in that, ideally, and you should be able to close the lid on it. That's a lot of manufacturing quality control. That is a lot of robots and cameras trying to figure out, does this actually work? Nobody wants to look at a Starbucks.

It's cupped every morning to see whether it's perfect. And so that is, I think, the biggest use case out there. I'm mentioning these two first because I think these are the real big ones. Humanoid robots, I know they get the most attention in the media because a dancing robot is always fun to watch. But that said...

There's just still a lot of technical challenges that we have to overcome in humanoid robots. It's true that if you put ChatGPT into a robot, there's this moment where you're amazed because suddenly this thing can see, it can talk to you, it can reason and argue back. But the problem is it still does these things fairly slowly. There's a little bit of a lag here, right? And it still needs to learn a lot of different things and it can very quickly be out of scope.

When you realize, ah, actually, this is a fantastic robot and it's learned how to cook, but it doesn't work in my kitchen. I've heard that, right, like, there's problems with renting Airbnbs in California these days because robotic companies are renting so many kitchens to train their cooking robots that you can't get an Airbnb in California anymore. It's probably exaggerated, but I think it's one of these funny anecdotes that just say that it's still quite early within robotics. That said...
There have been moments recently where I'm thinking we might be on the verge of a breakthrough. So the journey has begun.

And, you know, we have a long ways to go, but this is fascinating, Richard. We can have a separate episode just going deeper dive on this. For the interest of, for the sake of time, I'd love to kind of wrap this up right now and come up with, you know, your parting advice, share your parting advice to the viewers. If they're listening to you, what's next that they should do after this?

So I think the most interesting thing I've learned working in AI over the last eight years is that sometimes you have to get out of the AI space to make actual progress. Yeah. I know it's a little bit counterintuitive, but if you look at what engineering companies make the fastest progress, like SpaceX,

They actually do this in a very simplistic manner. They're like, let's go build this rocket, let's go test this rocket and see what happens, and then learn from this and iterate from this. I think engineers who love next generation tools, whether that is advanced simulation or AI, can get very swept up in, oh, I'm going to go and solve this with AI, and then

Don't realize that in many cases sometimes there can actually be a very simple and intuitive solution and a practical, right, like get out of the simulator and just check whether this thing drops when you sort of stop holding it, right, like can sometimes actually solve a lot of problems.

Awesome, awesome. So my closing thing would be just AI isn't replacing an engineer, it's giving them the superpowers to use it constructively and learn from it, test more and iterate more. Thank you everyone for joining us for this episode. We had a fantastic conversation with Richard. I'm sure you all will love listening to this and stay tuned for more. Thank you.
